questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between bond length and bond order in a series of carbon-carbon bonds?

choices.text: As bond order increases, bond length increases
choices.explanation: This is the reverse of the actual relationship; higher bond order draws atoms closer together.

choices.text: As bond order increases, bond length decreases
choices.explanation: Higher bond order means more electron density shared between nuclei, pulling them closer and shortening the bond.

choices.text: Bond length and bond order are independent of each other
choices.explanation: They are directly related; bond order determines how tightly the atoms are held together.

choices.text: As bond order increases, bond length first decreases then increases
choices.explanation: Bond length decreases monotonically with increasing bond order; there is no minimum-then-increase pattern.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: In a Lewis diagram for $\text{SO}_2$, which of the following best explains why resonance structures are drawn?

choices.text: The molecule rapidly oscillates between two distinct structures with alternating single and double bonds
choices.explanation: Resonance does not describe oscillation; the actual structure is a hybrid that does not switch between forms.

choices.text: The actual electron density is delocalized and cannot be represented by a single Lewis structure
choices.explanation: Resonance structures together indicate that electron density is spread across multiple bonds, producing an intermediate bond order.

choices.text: Sulfur can expand its octet, so one structure always has six bonds
choices.explanation: While expanded octets are possible, the reason for resonance is delocalization, not simply expanded octets.

choices.text: The two oxygen atoms are chemically inequivalent, requiring two different structures
choices.explanation: Resonance structures show that the oxygens are equivalent; the actual structure has equal bond lengths and orders.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

headers: Number of lone pairs on central atom

questions.question: Based on the table above, which of the following best explains why $\text{NH}_3$ has a smaller bond angle than $\text{BF}_3$?

choices.text: The nitrogen atom is smaller than boron, compressing the bond angles
choices.explanation: Atom size is not the primary explanation for bond angle differences in VSEPR theory.

choices.text: The lone pair on nitrogen repels the bonding pairs more strongly than bonding pairs repel each other
choices.explanation: Lone pair–bonding pair repulsions are stronger than bonding pair–bonding pair repulsions, compressing the H-N-H angle below 120°.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ has more bonds than $\text{BF}_3$, increasing repulsion between atoms
choices.explanation: Both molecules have three bonding pairs; the difference is the lone pair, not the bond count.

choices.text: The N–H bonds are polar, while B–F bonds are nonpolar, causing different geometries
choices.explanation: Bond polarity does not directly determine bond angles in VSEPR theory; electron pair repulsion is the determining factor.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following molecules has a tetrahedral electron geometry but a bent molecular geometry?

choices.text: $\text{CH}_4$
choices.explanation: $\text{CH}_4$ has four bonding pairs and no lone pairs, giving both tetrahedral electron geometry and tetrahedral molecular geometry.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$
choices.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$ has tetrahedral electron geometry but trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry, not bent.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
choices.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has four electron pairs (two bonding, two lone pairs) giving tetrahedral electron geometry, but the bent molecular geometry results from the two lone pairs.

choices.text: $\text{CO}_2$
choices.explanation: $\text{CO}_2$ has linear electron geometry and linear molecular geometry due to two double bonds and no lone pairs on carbon.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemist draws the Lewis structure for $\text{NO}_2^-$ (nitrite ion). She assigns formal charges to each atom.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the formal charge on the nitrogen atom in the Lewis structure of $\text{NO}_2^-$ that minimizes formal charges?

choices.explanation: A formal charge of $-1$ on nitrogen would require more electrons than are present in the minimized structure.
In the resonance structure where nitrogen forms one double bond and one single bond, nitrogen has 5 valence electrons and contributes 5, giving a formal charge of 0.
A $+1$ formal charge on nitrogen would appear in structures where nitrogen does not satisfy its normal bonding, which does not minimize formal charges here.
A formal charge of $+2$ on nitrogen is not consistent with any reasonable Lewis structure for nitrite.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why $\text{PCl}_5$ can exist but $\text{NCl}_5$ cannot?

choices.text: Phosphorus has a larger atomic radius than nitrogen, allowing more chlorine atoms to fit around it
choices.explanation: While size plays a role, the primary explanation is that phosphorus, as a period 3 element, can accommodate more than eight electrons due to its larger atomic radius and available energy levels in the third shell.

choices.text: Phosphorus can accommodate more than eight electrons due to its larger atomic radius and available energy levels in the third shell, while nitrogen cannot
choices.explanation: Period 3 elements like phosphorus have a larger atomic size and available energy levels that allow bonding with more than four atoms; period 2 elements like nitrogen are limited to the octet because only s and p orbitals are available in the second shell.

choices.text: Nitrogen is more electronegative than phosphorus and repels additional chlorine bonds
choices.explanation: Electronegativity differences do not prevent $\text{NCl}_5$ from forming; the octet limitation is the primary reason.

choices.text: Chlorine atoms are too large to form five bonds around nitrogen
choices.explanation: The size of chlorine relative to nitrogen plays a minor role; the fundamental constraint is nitrogen's inability to expand its octet.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is best supported?

choices.text: Bond energy is directly proportional to bond length for all bond types
choices.explanation: The relationship shows that bond energy increases as bond length decreases, which is an inverse relationship, not a direct one.

choices.text: Triple bonds always have higher bond energies than double bonds, which in turn have higher energies than single bonds
choices.explanation: The data consistently show that for both C–C and C–N bonds, triple > double > single in terms of bond energy, as more electron density is shared.

choices.text: Carbon–nitrogen bonds are always stronger than the corresponding carbon–carbon bonds
choices.explanation: C≡N (891 kJ/mol) is stronger than C≡C (839 kJ/mol), but C–N (305 kJ/mol) is weaker than C–C (347 kJ/mol).

choices.text: Bond length increases with bond order because more electrons create more repulsion
choices.explanation: Bond length decreases as bond order increases; higher bond order brings atoms closer, not farther apart.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student claims that $\text{CO}_2$ is a polar molecule because it contains two polar $\text{C=O}$ bonds. Which of the following best evaluates this claim?

choices.text: The claim is correct because polar bonds always produce a polar molecule
choices.explanation: Polar bonds do not necessarily produce a polar molecule; symmetrical arrangements can cancel bond dipoles.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect because $\text{CO}_2$ has a linear geometry in which the two bond dipoles cancel, yielding a nonpolar molecule
choices.explanation: The two C=O dipoles point in exactly opposite directions in the linear geometry, resulting in zero net dipole moment.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect because C=O bonds are nonpolar due to the similar electronegativities of carbon and oxygen
choices.explanation: Oxygen is significantly more electronegative than carbon, making C=O bonds polar; the molecule is nonpolar due to geometry, not bond polarity.

choices.text: The claim is correct because the carbon atom is between two oxygen atoms and cannot be symmetric
choices.explanation: Linear molecules can be perfectly symmetric; $\text{CO}_2$ is symmetric about the carbon atom, cancelling the dipoles.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Ozone ($\text{O}_3$) exhibits resonance and has bond angles of approximately 117°. The O–O bond length in ozone is 128 pm, which is between the lengths of a single O–O bond (148 pm) and a double O=O bond (121 pm).

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the O–O bond lengths in ozone are equal and intermediate between a single and double bond?

choices.text: The molecule rapidly switches between two Lewis structures, spending equal time in each
choices.explanation: The resonance hybrid is a single structure with delocalized electrons, not a molecule alternating between two forms.

choices.text: The electrons in the double bond are delocalized across both O–O bonds, creating bonds with an order of 1.5
choices.explanation: Delocalization of the pi electron density across both bonds produces equal, intermediate bond lengths corresponding to a bond order between 1 and 2.

choices.text: Ozone undergoes a rapid equilibrium reaction that converts single bonds to double bonds and vice versa
choices.explanation: This describes a chemical equilibrium, not resonance delocalization; ozone does not isomerize in this way.

choices.text: The bond length is intermediate because one bond is a sigma bond and the other is a pi bond
choices.explanation: Every covalent bond contains a sigma component; the intermediate length results from fractional bond order due to delocalization, not the sigma/pi distinction.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the hybridization of the central atom in $\text{SF}_6$?

choices.text: $sp$
choices.explanation: $sp$ hybridization produces a linear geometry with 2 electron groups; $\text{SF}_6$ has 6 bonding pairs around sulfur.
$sp^2$ hybridization produces a trigonal planar geometry with 3 electron groups; sulfur in $\text{SF}_6$ has 6 bonds.
$sp^3$ hybridization produces tetrahedral geometry with 4 electron groups; $\text{SF}_6$ has 6 bonding pairs, requiring $sp^3d^2$.
Six electron groups around sulfur require mixing one s, three p, and two d orbitals to form six equivalent $sp^3d^2$ hybrid orbitals in an octahedral arrangement.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of molecules are both nonpolar?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and $\text{CO}_2$
choices.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is polar (bent geometry, lone pairs create a net dipole); only $\text{CO}_2$ is nonpolar in this pair.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ and $\text{BF}_3$
choices.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$ is polar (trigonal pyramidal, net dipole from lone pair); $\text{BF}_3$ is nonpolar (trigonal planar, dipoles cancel).

choices.text: $\text{CCl}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$
choices.explanation: Both $\text{CCl}_4$ (tetrahedral, all identical bonds, dipoles cancel) and $\text{CO}_2$ (linear, identical bonds, dipoles cancel) are nonpolar.

choices.text: $\text{HCl}$ and $\text{Cl}_2$
choices.explanation: $\text{HCl}$ is polar (different atoms create a net dipole); only $\text{Cl}_2$ is nonpolar.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Bond Energies for Selected Carbon Bonds

questions.question: A chemist wants to break all the carbon–carbon bonds in a sample of ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$) and reform them as single bonds in ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$). Using the graph, which of the following best predicts the energy change for breaking the triple bond and forming a single bond?

choices.text: Energy is released because a single bond forms
choices.explanation: Whether energy is released or absorbed depends on the relative magnitudes; forming a single bond releases less energy than breaking a triple bond requires.

choices.text: Energy is absorbed because breaking the triple bond requires more energy than is released when forming the single bond
choices.explanation: Breaking C≡C requires 839 kJ/mol; forming C–C releases only 347 kJ/mol. The net process absorbs 839 – 347 = 492 kJ/mol.

choices.text: No net energy change occurs because the number of bonds is conserved
choices.explanation: Bond type, not just bond count, determines energy; replacing a triple bond with a single bond has a significant net energy cost.

choices.text: Energy is absorbed because single bonds are longer and therefore higher in energy
choices.explanation: Single bonds are lower in energy than triple bonds; the energy is absorbed because the triple bond energy exceeds the single bond energy.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the difference between a sigma ($\sigma$) bond and a pi ($\pi$) bond?

choices.text: Sigma bonds are formed by p orbitals only; pi bonds are formed by s orbitals only
choices.explanation: Sigma bonds can be formed by s or p orbitals overlapping end-to-end; pi bonds form by side-to-side p orbital overlap.

choices.text: Sigma bonds result from end-to-end orbital overlap along the internuclear axis; pi bonds result from side-to-side overlap of parallel p orbitals
choices.explanation: The defining characteristic is the geometry of overlap: sigma bonds have electron density along the bond axis; pi bonds have electron density above and below the axis.

choices.text: Sigma bonds are always stronger than pi bonds because they contain more electrons
choices.explanation: Sigma and pi bonds each contain 2 electrons; sigma bonds are generally stronger because end-to-end overlap is more effective than side-to-side overlap.

choices.text: Pi bonds can rotate freely around the bond axis; sigma bonds cannot
choices.explanation: It is sigma bonds that allow rotation; pi bonds restrict rotation because rotation would break the parallel orbital alignment needed for side-to-side overlap.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student examines the properties of two solids: Solid A melts at 1713°C and does not conduct electricity in any state. Solid B melts at 801°C and conducts electricity only when dissolved in water or melted.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the types of solids and explains the difference in melting points?

choices.text: Solid A is ionic; Solid B is covalent network. Ionic solids have stronger bonds.
choices.explanation: Solid A's very high melting point and non-conductivity are characteristic of a covalent network solid, not an ionic solid.

choices.text: Solid A is covalent network ($\text{SiO}_2$); Solid B is ionic ($\text{NaCl}$). Covalent network solids require breaking covalent bonds, which demands more energy than disrupting ionic lattices.
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids like $\text{SiO}_2$ require breaking many strong covalent bonds throughout the lattice, resulting in very high melting points. Ionic solids like $\text{NaCl}$ require overcoming electrostatic attractions, which is significant but generally less demanding.

choices.text: Solid A is molecular; Solid B is metallic. Metallic solids have weaker bonds.
choices.explanation: Molecular solids typically have low melting points due to weak intermolecular forces; the 1713°C melting point is inconsistent with a molecular solid.

choices.text: Both solids are ionic, but Solid A has higher charge ions, increasing lattice energy.
choices.explanation: Solid A does not conduct electricity in any state, ruling out an ionic solid (which conducts when molten).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: In which of the following molecules does the central atom have an expanded octet?

choices.text: $\text{BF}_3$
choices.explanation: Boron in $\text{BF}_3$ has only 6 electrons around it (an incomplete octet), not an expanded octet.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4^+$
choices.explanation: Nitrogen in $\text{NH}_4^+$ has exactly 8 electrons (octet), not an expanded octet.

choices.text: $\text{PCl}_5$
choices.explanation: Phosphorus in $\text{PCl}_5$ has 10 electrons around it (5 bonds × 2 electrons = 10), which exceeds the normal octet.

choices.text: $\text{CF}_4$
choices.explanation: Carbon in $\text{CF}_4$ has exactly 8 electrons (4 bonds × 2 electrons = 8), satisfying but not exceeding the octet.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, a student proposes that lone pairs always decrease bond angles compared to an ideal tetrahedral angle. Which of the following best evaluates this claim?

choices.text: The claim is fully supported; every lone pair reduces the bond angle by a fixed amount
choices.explanation: The reduction is not fixed; $\text{NH}_3$ and $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ both have 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair but have different bond angles, showing the reduction varies.

choices.text: The claim is partially supported; lone pairs generally decrease bond angles, but the effect varies with the electronegativity of surrounding atoms and charge on the central atom
choices.explanation: $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ has a lone pair but a larger angle (113°) than $\text{NH}_3$ (107°); the positive charge on $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ reduces lone pair repulsion, illustrating that the effect is not uniform.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect; lone pairs always increase bond angles because they repel bonding pairs outward
choices.explanation: Lone pairs do repel bonding pairs outward, but this compresses the angle between bonding pairs, not increases it.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect because $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ has the same lone pair count as $\text{NH}_3$ but a larger bond angle, showing lone pairs increase bond angles
choices.explanation: The larger angle in $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ is due to the positive charge reducing electron-electron repulsion, not an inherent bond-angle-increasing effect of lone pairs.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct Lewis structure consideration for the $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ ion?

choices.text: The ion has three equivalent C–O bonds due to resonance, each with a bond order of 1.33
choices.explanation: Three resonance structures each place the double bond at a different oxygen. The hybrid has three equal bonds, each with bond order = 4/3 ≈ 1.33.

choices.text: The ion has one C=O double bond and two C–O single bonds, giving three inequivalent bonds
choices.explanation: A single Lewis structure shows this, but the actual structure is a resonance hybrid with three equivalent bonds.

choices.text: The ion has three C=O double bonds, using all available electrons
choices.explanation: Three double bonds would require more electrons than are available in $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$; only one double bond (or its resonance equivalent) can be drawn at a time.

choices.text: The two extra electrons from the $2-$ charge are located entirely on one oxygen atom
choices.explanation: The extra electron density is delocalized across all three oxygens in the resonance hybrid, not localized on one atom.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the structure of diamond that explains its hardness?

choices.text: Diamond contains delocalized electrons throughout a metallic lattice, allowing strong metallic bonding
choices.explanation: Diamond is a covalent network solid, not metallic; it has no free electrons (and does not conduct electricity).

choices.text: Diamond is a molecular solid with very strong dispersion forces between large molecules
choices.explanation: Diamond is not a collection of discrete molecules; it is an extended covalent network of C–C bonds throughout the entire crystal.

choices.text: Each carbon atom forms four strong covalent bonds in a tetrahedral arrangement, creating an extended three-dimensional covalent network
choices.explanation: The $sp^3$-hybridized carbons form a rigid, three-dimensional lattice of C–C bonds; hardness results from the need to break strong covalent bonds to deform the structure.

choices.text: Diamond contains ionic bonds between carbon cations and carbon anions in a crystal lattice
choices.explanation: Carbon does not typically form stable ionic species under normal conditions; diamond consists entirely of covalent C–C bonds.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Graphite and diamond are both allotropes of carbon, yet they have dramatically different physical properties.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why graphite conducts electricity but diamond does not?

choices.text: Graphite has metallic bonding; diamond has covalent bonding
choices.explanation: Both graphite and diamond have covalent bonding; neither has metallic bonding in the traditional sense.

choices.text: In graphite, each carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized with delocalized pi electrons between layers; in diamond, each carbon is $sp^3$ with all electrons in localized sigma bonds
choices.explanation: The unhybridized p orbital on each $sp^2$ carbon in graphite overlaps to form delocalized pi electrons that can carry charge; diamond's $sp^3$ carbons have no mobile electrons.

choices.text: Graphite has more electrons per carbon atom than diamond does
choices.explanation: Both are pure carbon with the same number of electrons per atom; the difference lies in the orbital hybridization and electron delocalization.

choices.text: Diamond molecules are larger than graphite molecules, limiting electron flow
choices.explanation: Both graphite and diamond are extended network solids, not discrete molecules; the conductivity difference is due to electron delocalization.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: What is the molecular geometry of $\text{XeF}_4$?

choices.text: Tetrahedral
choices.explanation: Tetrahedral geometry arises from 4 electron groups with no lone pairs; $\text{XeF}_4$ has 6 electron groups (4 bonds + 2 lone pairs).

choices.text: Seesaw
choices.explanation: Seesaw geometry results from 5 electron groups with 1 lone pair; $\text{XeF}_4$ has 6 electron groups (2 lone pairs, 4 bonds).

choices.text: Square planar
choices.explanation: With 6 total electron groups (4 bonding, 2 lone pairs), the lone pairs occupy axial positions opposite each other in an octahedral arrangement, leaving the four fluorines in a square planar geometry.

choices.text: Octahedral
choices.explanation: Octahedral molecular geometry requires 6 bonding pairs with no lone pairs; $\text{XeF}_4$ has 2 lone pairs that must be accounted for in its molecular geometry.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student tests an unknown solid and finds it has a melting point of 320°C and does not conduct electricity in the solid state but does conduct electricity when dissolved in water. Which solid type in the table does the unknown solid most likely resemble?

choices.text: Molecular solid ($\text{I}_2$)
choices.explanation: Molecular solids dissolved in water generally do not produce conducting solutions because they do not dissociate into ions.

choices.text: Covalent network solid ($\text{SiO}_2$)
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids do not conduct electricity in any state, nor do they typically dissolve to form conducting aqueous solutions.

choices.text: Ionic crystal ($\text{NaCl}$)
choices.explanation: The unknown solid conducts when dissolved, indicating it dissociates into ions in solution. Moderate melting point is consistent with ionic solids of lower lattice energy than $\text{NaCl}$.

choices.text: Metallic solid ($\text{Fe}$)
choices.explanation: Metallic solids conduct electricity in the solid state; the unknown solid does not, ruling out a metallic solid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the bond angle in $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ ($92^\circ$) is smaller than that in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ($104.5^\circ$)?

choices.text: Sulfur has more lone pairs than oxygen, creating greater repulsion
choices.explanation: Both $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ have two lone pairs on the central atom.

choices.text: The S–H bond is longer than the O–H bond, pushing the hydrogen atoms farther apart
choices.explanation: Longer bonds place the H atoms farther from the central atom but do not necessarily compress the bond angle; bond polarity and orbital character are better explanations.

choices.text: Sulfur uses orbitals with more p character for bonding, reducing repulsion between bonding pairs and allowing the angle to compress toward $90^\circ$
choices.explanation: Sulfur uses nearly pure p orbitals for bonding, which are mutually perpendicular (90°), while oxygen uses more $sp^3$-hybridized orbitals, producing a larger angle.

choices.text: Sulfur is more electronegative than oxygen, drawing bonding electrons toward itself and compressing the angle
choices.explanation: Oxygen is more electronegative than sulfur; this explanation is factually incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student calculates the formal charge on oxygen in water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$). Using the formula: Formal charge = (valence electrons) − (lone pair electrons) − ½(bonding electrons), what is the formal charge on oxygen in water?

choices.explanation: $-2$ would mean oxygen has 2 more electrons than its valence count; in water, the formal charge on oxygen is 0, not $-2$.
$-1$ would apply if oxygen had 7 electrons assigned; in water, oxygen has 4 lone pair + ½(4 bonding) = 4 + 2 = 6 = valence count, giving 0.
Oxygen has 6 valence electrons. In water: 4 lone pair electrons + ½(4 bonding electrons) = 4 + 2 = 6. Formal charge = 6 − 6 = 0.
A formal charge of $+1$ would mean oxygen contributes more electrons to bonds than it possesses; this occurs in $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$, not neutral $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Two chemists argue about the polarity of $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ (chloromethane). Chemist A says it is polar. Chemist B says it should be nonpolar because the tetrahedral geometry makes the dipoles cancel.

questions.question: Which chemist is correct, and what is the best explanation?

choices.text: Chemist B is correct; tetrahedral molecules always have canceling dipoles and are therefore nonpolar
choices.explanation: Dipoles in a tetrahedral molecule only cancel when all four bonds are identical (e.g., $\text{CCl}_4$); different substituents create a net dipole.

choices.text: Chemist A is correct; the C–Cl bond is more polar than the three C–H bonds, and the asymmetry creates a net molecular dipole
choices.explanation: The four bonds in $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ are not all identical; the C–Cl bond dipole is not cancelled by three C–H dipoles, resulting in a net polar molecule.

choices.text: Both chemists are wrong; $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ is nonpolar because carbon is at the center
choices.explanation: The centrality of carbon does not determine polarity; the identity and arrangement of the surrounding atoms does.

choices.text: Chemist A is correct; tetrahedral molecules are always polar because the central atom always has an asymmetric environment
choices.explanation: $\text{CCl}_4$ is a tetrahedral molecule that is nonpolar because all four bonds are identical and the dipoles cancel.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the trend in bond polarity for the series $\text{H–F}$, $\text{H–Cl}$, $\text{H–Br}$, $\text{H–I}$?

choices.text: Bond polarity increases from H–F to H–I because iodine is larger and can accommodate more electrons
choices.explanation: Polarity decreases down the series; iodine's lower electronegativity relative to hydrogen means the H–I bond is less polar than H–F.

choices.text: Bond polarity decreases from H–F to H–I because electronegativity of the halogen decreases down the group
choices.explanation: Electronegativity decreases going down Group VIIA (F > Cl > Br > I), so the electronegativity difference with hydrogen decreases, reducing bond polarity.

choices.text: All four bonds have approximately equal polarity because hydrogen bonds equally with all halogens
choices.explanation: The halogens have significantly different electronegativities; H–F is far more polar than H–I.

choices.text: Bond polarity first decreases and then increases because of the interplay between electronegativity and atomic radius
choices.explanation: The trend is monotonic; polarity decreases steadily from H–F to H–I as electronegativity decreases down the group.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains why MgO has a significantly larger lattice energy magnitude than NaCl?

choices.text: MgO has more ions per formula unit than NaCl, increasing the number of attractive interactions
choices.explanation: Both MgO and NaCl have one cation and one anion per formula unit; the difference in lattice energy is due to charge and ionic radius, not ion count.

choices.text: The $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions have higher charges and smaller radii than $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$, producing stronger electrostatic attraction
choices.explanation: Lattice energy is proportional to the product of ion charges and inversely proportional to the sum of ionic radii. MgO has doubly charged ions that are also smaller, dramatically increasing lattice energy.

choices.text: MgO has a different crystal structure than NaCl, which always produces higher lattice energies
choices.explanation: Crystal structure plays a role, but the dominant factor is ion charge and radius; CaO with the same NaCl structure has a high lattice energy too.

choices.text: Mg is a transition metal with d electrons that contribute additional bonding
choices.explanation: Magnesium is an alkaline earth metal (Group 2), not a transition metal, and its lattice energy advantage is due to its +2 charge and small ionic radius.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student draws a Lewis structure for $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ (nitrous oxide). The molecule is linear with the connectivity N–N–O.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of formal charges best represents the preferred Lewis structure of $\text{N}_2\text{O}$?

choices.text: Left N: $-1$, middle N: $+1$, O: $0$
choices.explanation: This is one possible resonance contributor but does not minimize formal charges as effectively as the structure with a triple bond on N–N and single bond to O.

choices.text: Left N: $-1$, middle N: $+1$, O: $0$ — this is the only valid structure
choices.explanation: Multiple resonance structures exist for N₂O; the preferred one minimizes the magnitude of formal charges.

choices.text: Left N: $0$, middle N: $+1$, O: $-1$
choices.explanation: The structure $:N\equiv N^+-O^{-}:$ places formal charges of 0, +1, and -1 on left N, middle N, and O respectively, minimizing magnitudes and placing negative charge on the more electronegative oxygen.

choices.text: All atoms have formal charge of $0$
choices.explanation: A structure with all zero formal charges would require nitrogen to form bonds inconsistent with normal valences; the preferred structure has formal charges of 0, +1, and -1.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Using the data in the table, which of the following predictions about $\text{SF}_6$ is best supported?

choices.text: $\text{SF}_6$ has a bond angle of $109.5^\circ$ and tetrahedral molecular geometry
choices.explanation: Tetrahedral geometry arises from 4 electron groups; SF₆ has 6 bonding pairs around sulfur.

choices.text: $\text{SF}_6$ has bond angles of $90^\circ$ and octahedral molecular geometry with 6 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs
choices.explanation: Following the pattern in the table, 6 electron groups with no lone pairs produces octahedral geometry with 90° bond angles.

choices.text: $\text{SF}_6$ has bond angles of $120^\circ$ and trigonal bipyramidal geometry
choices.explanation: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has 5 electron groups; SF₆ has 6.

choices.text: $\text{SF}_6$ cannot be predicted from the table because sulfur has d orbitals
choices.explanation: VSEPR theory applies regardless of d orbital involvement; the table's pattern of increasing electron groups predicts the octahedral arrangement.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the three compounds in order of increasing lattice energy magnitude: $\text{LiF}$, $\text{NaF}$, $\text{KF}$?

choices.text: $\text{LiF} < \text{NaF} < \text{KF}$
choices.explanation: Lattice energy increases as ionic radius decreases; LiF has the smallest cation and therefore the largest lattice energy magnitude, not the smallest.

choices.text: $\text{KF} < \text{NaF} < \text{LiF}$
choices.explanation: Lattice energy is inversely proportional to interionic distance. Li⁺ is the smallest cation, giving the shortest Li–F distance and the largest lattice energy. K⁺ is largest, giving the smallest lattice energy.

choices.text: $\text{NaF} < \text{KF} < \text{LiF}$
choices.explanation: Lattice energy increases as cation radius decreases; NaF should be intermediate, not smallest.

choices.text: All three compounds have the same lattice energy because they all have 1:1 charge ratios
choices.explanation: Same charge ratio does not mean same lattice energy; interionic distance (controlled by ion size) also determines lattice energy.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: An unknown compound X has the following properties: it is a solid at room temperature, melts at 3,550°C, is extremely hard, and does not conduct electricity in any state.

questions.question: Which of the following best identifies the type of solid compound X is and the bonding that explains its properties?

choices.text: Ionic solid; the very high charge density of the ions creates an extremely strong lattice
choices.explanation: Ionic solids do conduct electricity when melted; compound X does not conduct in any state, ruling out ionic bonding.

choices.text: Metallic solid; the sea of delocalized electrons is very constrained in this metal
choices.explanation: Metallic solids conduct electricity in all states due to mobile electrons; compound X does not conduct, ruling out metallic bonding.

choices.text: Covalent network solid; the extreme hardness, very high melting point, and non-conductivity are characteristic of an extended network of strong covalent bonds (e.g., diamond)
choices.explanation: Diamond melts at ~3,550°C and is the hardest natural substance; both properties result from the three-dimensional network of strong C–C covalent bonds that must all be broken to melt or deform the solid.

choices.text: Molecular solid; very large molecules can have strong dispersion forces explaining the high melting point
choices.explanation: Molecular solids rely on intermolecular forces (weaker than covalent bonds) and melt at much lower temperatures; 3,550°C is far beyond any molecular solid's melting range.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Lattice Energies of Selected Ionic Compounds

questions.question: Based on the bar chart, which of the following conclusions about lattice energy is best supported by the data?

choices.text: Lattice energy decreases as ionic radius increases, because larger ions are farther apart and have weaker electrostatic attraction
choices.explanation: The Na halide series (NaF → NaCl → NaBr → NaI) shows decreasing lattice energy as the anion radius increases, increasing interionic distance and weakening electrostatic attraction.

choices.text: Lattice energy is determined solely by the anion charge, so all sodium salts should have equal lattice energies
choices.explanation: The data clearly show NaF, NaCl, NaBr, and NaI have different lattice energies despite all having 1- anions; ionic radius plays a significant role.

choices.text: LiF has the highest lattice energy because fluorine is the most electronegative element
choices.explanation: LiF has the highest lattice energy because Li⁺ is the smallest cation, not because of fluorine's electronegativity per se; lattice energy depends on ion charge and radius.

choices.text: NaI has the lowest lattice energy because iodine has fewer electrons than fluorine
choices.explanation: Iodine has more electrons than fluorine. The lower lattice energy of NaI is due to I⁻ being the largest halide ion, not fewer electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following molecules is polar and has a trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry?

choices.text: $\text{BF}_3$
choices.explanation: $\text{BF}_3$ is trigonal planar (no lone pairs on B) and nonpolar because the three B–F dipoles cancel symmetrically.

choices.text: $\text{NF}_3$
choices.explanation: $\text{NF}_3$ has three bonding pairs and one lone pair on nitrogen, giving trigonal pyramidal geometry. The N–F bond dipoles do not cancel because the lone pair creates an asymmetric arrangement, resulting in a net dipole moment.

choices.text: $\text{CF}_4$
choices.explanation: $\text{CF}_4$ has tetrahedral molecular geometry (4 bonding pairs, no lone pairs) and is nonpolar because the four C–F dipoles cancel in the symmetric tetrahedral arrangement.

choices.text: $\text{XeF}_4$
choices.explanation: $\text{XeF}_4$ is square planar with two lone pairs, and is nonpolar because the four Xe–F bond dipoles cancel in the symmetric square planar arrangement.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student is asked to draw Lewis structures for $\text{SO}_3$ and $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ (sulfite ion). The student notes that adding electrons changes the geometry.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes how the electron geometry and molecular geometry differ between $\text{SO}_3$ and $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$?

choices.text: $\text{SO}_3$ is trigonal planar; $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ is trigonal pyramidal because the added electrons become a lone pair on sulfur
choices.explanation: $\text{SO}_3$ has three double bonds and no lone pairs on S (trigonal planar). $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ has an additional electron pair on S, giving it tetrahedral electron geometry and trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry.

choices.text: Both $\text{SO}_3$ and $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ are trigonal planar because sulfur always forms three bonds
choices.explanation: The additional electrons in $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ create a lone pair on sulfur, changing the electron geometry from trigonal planar to tetrahedral.

choices.text: $\text{SO}_3$ is trigonal pyramidal; $\text{SO}_3^{2-}$ is trigonal planar because the added charge removes lone pairs
choices.explanation: This reverses the actual relationship; SO₃ is planar and SO₃²⁻ is pyramidal.

choices.text: Both are tetrahedral because sulfur has d orbitals that accommodate extra electrons
choices.explanation: Tetrahedral molecular geometry requires 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs; neither SO₃ nor SO₃²⁻ has this arrangement.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student claims that all aluminum compounds are ionic because aluminum is a metal. Which of the following best evaluates this claim using the data?

choices.text: The claim is correct; all aluminum compounds in the table are ionic
choices.explanation: AlCl₃ sublimes at 192°C and has poor conductivity, suggesting significant covalent character, not a purely ionic compound.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect; $\text{AlCl}_3$ shows covalent character because it sublimes at a low temperature and has poor electrical conductivity
choices.explanation: Ionic compounds typically have high melting/boiling points and conduct when molten. AlCl₃'s low sublimation point and poor conductivity indicate substantial covalent character, as Al is a small, highly polarizing cation.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect; all aluminum compounds are covalent network solids
choices.explanation: Al₂O₃ is ionic (conducts when molten, high melting point), not a covalent network solid.

choices.text: The claim is correct because aluminum's high melting point as a metal indicates strong metallic bonding that extends to its compounds
choices.explanation: The bonding in aluminum metal does not determine the bonding type in aluminum compounds; each compound must be evaluated independently.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the number of sigma and pi bonds in a molecule of ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$)?

choices.text: 4 sigma bonds and 1 pi bond
choices.explanation: There are 4 C–H sigma bonds plus 1 C–C sigma bond = 5 sigma bonds, and 1 pi bond in the C=C double bond.

choices.text: 5 sigma bonds and 1 pi bond
choices.explanation: Ethene has four C–H sigma bonds and one C–C sigma bond (part of the double bond) = 5 sigma bonds, plus one pi bond from the second component of the C=C double bond.

choices.text: 4 sigma bonds and 2 pi bonds
choices.explanation: Ethene has a double bond (1 sigma + 1 pi) between the carbons, and four C–H sigma bonds. There is only one pi bond, not two.

choices.text: 6 sigma bonds and 0 pi bonds
choices.explanation: A C=C double bond always contains one sigma and one pi bond; counting 6 sigma bonds and no pi bonds would imply only single bonds, which contradicts the double bond in ethene.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the compounds MgO, NaCl, and CsCl in order of **increasing** melting point?

choices.text: $	ext{CsCl} < 	ext{NaCl} < 	ext{MgO}$
$	ext{NaCl} < 	ext{CsCl} < 	ext{MgO}$
$	ext{MgO} < 	ext{NaCl} < 	ext{CsCl}$
$	ext{CsCl} < 	ext{MgO} < 	ext{NaCl}$

questions.explanation: Lattice energy ∝ (charge product)/(ionic radius). MgO: $2^+ 	imes 2^- = 4$, small ions → highest lattice energy → highest melting point (2852°C). NaCl: $1^+ 	imes 1^-$, moderate size (801°C). CsCl: $1^+ 	imes 1^-$, large Cs$^+$ → weakest lattice energy → lowest melting point (646°C). Order: CsCl < NaCl < MgO.

choice_explanations.A: Correct. Coulomb's law: higher charge and smaller radius → stronger lattice → higher melting point.
choice_explanations.B: Incorrect. Cs$^+$ is larger than Na$^+$, giving CsCl a lower melting point than NaCl.
choice_explanations.C: Incorrect. This inverts the relationship; MgO has by far the strongest lattice due to 2+ and 2- charges.
choice_explanations.D: Incorrect. NaCl has a higher melting point than CsCl due to smaller ionic radius.

questions.question: How many total valence electrons are present in the Lewis structure of $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (sulfate ion)?

choices.explanation: S has 6 valence electrons + 4×6 for O = 30, but the 2- charge adds 2 more electrons, giving 32 total.
S contributes 6 valence electrons, four O atoms contribute 4 × 6 = 24, and the 2– charge contributes 2 additional electrons: 6 + 24 + 2 = 32 total valence electrons.
34 would result from adding too many electrons; the 2– charge adds only 2 electrons to the neutral atom total of 30.
28 would result from subtracting electrons instead of adding them for the negative charge, or from miscounting O valence electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student investigates why some molecules with polar bonds are nonpolar overall. She considers four molecules: $\text{CCl}_4$, $\text{CHCl}_3$, $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$, and $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the nonpolar molecule(s) among those described and explains why?

choices.text: $\text{CCl}_4$ only; the four identical C–Cl bond dipoles point symmetrically outward and cancel completely
choices.explanation: In CCl₄, all four bonds are identical C–Cl bonds arranged tetrahedrally; the vector sum of the four bond dipoles is zero, making the molecule nonpolar despite having polar bonds.

choices.text: $\text{CCl}_4$ and $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$; both have symmetrical arrangements
choices.explanation: CH₂Cl₂ has two C–H and two C–Cl bonds; the C–Cl dipoles are stronger than C–H dipoles, so they do not cancel, making CH₂Cl₂ polar.

choices.text: All four molecules are nonpolar because carbon is at the center and balances all dipoles
choices.explanation: The centrality of carbon alone does not cancel dipoles; only when all surrounding bonds are identical do the dipoles cancel.

choices.text: None of the molecules is nonpolar because all contain C–Cl bonds, which are polar
choices.explanation: The presence of polar bonds does not guarantee a polar molecule; if the bond dipoles cancel due to molecular symmetry, the molecule is nonpolar.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly lists the hybridization of the central atoms in $\text{BF}_3$, $\text{CH}_4$, and $\text{SF}_6$, respectively?

choices.explanation: BF₃ has 3 electron groups ($sp^2$); CH₄ has 4 electron groups ($sp^3$); SF₆ has 6 electron groups ($sp^3d^2$). Each hybridization matches the number of electron groups around the central atom.
BF₃ has 3 electron groups corresponding to $sp^2$, not $sp^3$; CH₄ is $sp^3$, not $sp^2$; SF₆ requires $sp^3d^2$ for 6 groups.
BF₃ has 3 electron groups requiring $sp^2$ hybridization, not $sp$ (which is for 2 groups).
SF₆ has 6 electron groups requiring $sp^3d^2$ hybridization, not $sp^3d$ (which is for 5 groups).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Two ionic compounds are compared: $\text{MgO}$ (lattice energy = $-3795$ kJ/mol) and $\text{NaCl}$ (lattice energy = $-787$ kJ/mol). A student predicts that MgO should have a much higher melting point than NaCl.

questions.question: Which of the following best supports the student's prediction?

choices.text: The student is correct; MgO melts at approximately 2852°C while NaCl melts at 801°C, consistent with MgO's much larger lattice energy
choices.explanation: The lattice energy of MgO is nearly five times that of NaCl, reflecting the higher charges (+2 and -2 vs +1 and -1) and smaller ionic radii. More energy is needed to overcome these stronger attractions, resulting in a much higher melting point.

choices.text: The student is incorrect; melting point depends on molecular weight, not lattice energy, and NaCl has a higher molecular weight
choices.explanation: For ionic solids, lattice energy (not molecular weight) primarily determines melting point; NaCl (58.44 g/mol) is actually lighter than MgO (40.30 g/mol), yet NaCl melts lower, contradicting the molecular weight hypothesis.

choices.text: The student's prediction cannot be evaluated without knowing the crystal structures
choices.explanation: While crystal structure affects the Madelung constant, the dominant factor is the large difference in lattice energies (nearly 5×), which reliably predicts MgO's higher melting point.

choices.text: The student is correct, but only because Mg has a higher atomic mass than Na
choices.explanation: Atomic mass does not determine lattice energy or melting point in ionic compounds; the charge of the ions is the primary driver.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the bonding in a metallic solid such as copper?

choices.text: Copper atoms share electron pairs in localized covalent bonds arranged in a lattice
choices.explanation: Metallic bonding is not localized; the electrons are delocalized (mobile) throughout the entire metallic crystal, which is what gives metals their conductivity and malleability.

choices.text: Copper consists of $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{Cu}^-$ ions arranged in an alternating lattice, held together by electrostatic attraction
choices.explanation: Metals do not form alternating positive and negative ion lattices; metallic bonding involves cations immersed in a sea of delocalized electrons.

choices.text: Copper atoms form a regular array of cations surrounded by a sea of delocalized valence electrons that attract all cations simultaneously
choices.explanation: Metallic bonding is characterized by metal cations held in a crystal lattice by a 'sea' of freely moving (delocalized) electrons. This model explains electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and malleability.

choices.text: Copper atoms are held together by strong dispersion forces between large electron clouds
choices.explanation: Dispersion forces are intermolecular forces between neutral atoms or molecules; metallic copper is held together by metallic bonding (delocalized electrons), not dispersion forces.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the patterns in the table, what molecular geometry would be predicted for $\text{IF}_7$ (7 bonds, 0 lone pairs on iodine)?

choices.text: Octahedral, because 7 is close to 6 bonds
choices.explanation: Octahedral geometry requires exactly 6 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs; IF₇ has 7 bonding pairs, which gives a different geometry.

choices.text: Pentagonal bipyramidal, because 7 electron groups with no lone pairs arrange in this geometry
choices.explanation: Seven electron groups with no lone pairs adopt a pentagonal bipyramidal geometry to minimize repulsion. This is consistent with VSEPR theory for 7-coordinate molecules like IF₇.

choices.text: Trigonal bipyramidal, because iodine typically forms 5 bonds
choices.explanation: IF₇ has 7 bonds (not 5), so trigonal bipyramidal geometry (which requires 5 electron groups) does not apply.

choices.text: Tetrahedral, because lone pairs on the outer fluorines determine the geometry
choices.explanation: Molecular geometry is determined by the arrangement of atoms around the central atom, not by lone pairs on terminal atoms.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why $\text{KCl}$ dissolves in water but $\text{CaCO}_3$ does not dissolve appreciably in water?

choices.text: KCl has a lower lattice energy than $\text{CaCO}_3$; when lattice energy is lower than hydration energy, dissolution is favored
choices.explanation: KCl has a relatively low lattice energy (~715 kJ/mol) that is overcome by the hydration energy of K⁺ and Cl⁻. CaCO₃ has a very high lattice energy (~2804 kJ/mol) because of the doubly charged ions, which is not sufficiently compensated by hydration energy.

choices.text: KCl is a molecular compound that easily disperses in water; $\text{CaCO}_3$ is ionic and repels water molecules
choices.explanation: KCl is an ionic compound, not molecular. Both compounds are ionic; the difference is in the relative magnitudes of lattice energy and hydration energy.

choices.text: $\text{CaCO}_3$ is too large to dissolve in water; KCl has smaller ions that fit easily in solution
choices.explanation: Ion size alone does not determine solubility; the balance between lattice energy and hydration energy is the determining factor.

choices.text: Water molecules are too small to surround the $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ ion effectively
choices.explanation: Water can and does solvate carbonate ions; however, the very high lattice energy of CaCO₃ is not overcome by the hydration energy, making it sparingly soluble.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemistry teacher asks students to identify which of the following molecules contains a coordinate covalent (dative) bond: $\text{NH}_4^+$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$, or $\text{CO}_2$.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies a coordinate covalent bond and explains its formation?

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$; the triple bond contains one coordinate covalent bond where one nitrogen donates both electrons
choices.explanation: In N₂, each nitrogen atom contributes one electron to each bond; the bonding is equally shared, not coordinate covalent.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4^+$; the bond between nitrogen and the fourth hydrogen is coordinate covalent, formed when the nitrogen lone pair bonds to $H^+$
choices.explanation: In forming NH₄⁺, nitrogen donates both electrons of its lone pair to H⁺ (which has no electrons), creating a coordinate covalent bond. Once formed, this bond is indistinguishable from the other N–H bonds.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; the oxygen lone pairs form coordinate bonds with hydrogen
choices.explanation: In water, each O–H bond is formed by one electron from oxygen and one from hydrogen; the lone pairs on oxygen are not used to form bonds in the water molecule itself.

choices.text: $\text{CO}_2$; the two double bonds are coordinate covalent because carbon donates all electrons
choices.explanation: In CO₂, both carbon and oxygen contribute electrons to the bonding; these are not coordinate covalent bonds where one atom donates both electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the molecular geometry and polarity of $\text{SCl}_2$?

choices.text: Linear and nonpolar, because S is in the center
choices.explanation: Linear geometry would require 0 or 2 lone pairs on the central atom with no additional groups; S in SCl₂ has 2 lone pairs and 2 bonding pairs, giving bent geometry.

choices.text: Bent and polar, because the two lone pairs on S create an asymmetric arrangement of bond dipoles
choices.explanation: SCl₂ has the same electron geometry as H₂O: 4 electron groups (2 bonding, 2 lone pairs) giving tetrahedral electron geometry and bent molecular geometry. The two S–Cl bond dipoles do not cancel, making the molecule polar.

choices.text: Bent and nonpolar, because the two S–Cl bond dipoles cancel each other
choices.explanation: For two bond dipoles to cancel, the molecule would need to be linear (180° bond angle). In a bent molecule with ~103° bond angle, the dipoles do not cancel.

choices.text: Trigonal planar and nonpolar, because sulfur has 3 electron groups
choices.explanation: Sulfur in SCl₂ has 4 electron groups (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs), not 3; the geometry is bent, not trigonal planar.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is best supported about the relationship between bond order, bond length, and bond energy for oxygen species?

choices.text: As bond order increases, both bond length and bond energy increase
choices.explanation: The data show bond energy increases with bond order, but bond length decreases with bond order — opposite directions.

choices.text: As bond order increases, bond length decreases and bond energy increases, consistent with stronger, shorter bonds
choices.explanation: From O₂²⁻ (BO=1) to O₂⁺ (BO=2.5), bond length decreases from 149 pm to 112 pm while bond energy increases from 204 to 625 kJ/mol — directly demonstrating the inverse length/bond order and direct energy/bond order relationships.

choices.text: Bond length and bond energy are independent of bond order for oxygen species
choices.explanation: The data clearly show systematic trends: all three quantities (bond order, bond length, bond energy) are directly related.

choices.text: Charged oxygen species always have higher bond energies than neutral $\text{O}_2$
choices.explanation: $\text{O}_2^-$ (395 kJ/mol) and $\text{O}_2^{2-}$ (204 kJ/mol) both have lower bond energies than neutral O₂ (498 kJ/mol).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between electronegativity difference and bond type for a diatomic molecule?

choices.text: A difference of 0 always indicates an ionic bond
choices.explanation: An electronegativity difference of 0 indicates equal sharing of electrons, which is a nonpolar covalent bond (e.g., H₂ or Cl₂).

choices.text: A difference greater than 1.7 is generally considered ionic, while a difference of 0.5–1.7 indicates polar covalent, and less than 0.5 indicates nonpolar covalent
choices.explanation: These threshold values are the standard AP Chemistry guidelines: ΔEN < 0.5 → nonpolar covalent; 0.5–1.7 → polar covalent; > 1.7 → predominantly ionic character.

choices.text: Any nonzero electronegativity difference results in an ionic bond
choices.explanation: A small electronegativity difference (e.g., C–H with ΔEN ≈ 0.4) results in a nonpolar or slightly polar covalent bond, not an ionic bond.

choices.text: Electronegativity difference is irrelevant to bond type; only atom size determines whether a bond is ionic or covalent
choices.explanation: Electronegativity difference is the primary predictor of bond type; atom size plays a secondary role through polarizability.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student investigates the Lewis structure of $\text{NO}^+$ (nitrosonium ion). Nitrogen has 5 valence electrons and oxygen has 6 valence electrons.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Lewis structure of $\text{NO}^+$ and the formal charges on each atom?

choices.text: A triple bond between N and O; N has formal charge $+1$ and O has formal charge $0$
choices.explanation: This swaps the formal charges. With a triple bond and one lone pair on each atom: N FC = 5 − 2 − 3 = 0 (not +1); O FC = 6 − 2 − 3 = +1 (not 0). Oxygen is more electronegative, but formal charge is determined by electron bookkeeping, not electronegativity.

choices.text: A triple bond between N and O; N has formal charge $0$ and O has formal charge $0$, with all electrons accounted for
choices.explanation: With a triple bond in NO⁺, there are 10 electrons: 6 in the triple bond and 2 lone pairs on each atom (2+2=4). N: 5-2-3=0; O: 6-2-3=+1. Oxygen carries the +1 formal charge, not 0.

choices.text: A double bond between N and O; N has formal charge $+1$ and O has formal charge $0$
choices.explanation: A double bond in NO⁺ would leave 6 remaining electrons (3 lone pairs total). N: 5-4-2=−1; O: 6-4-2=0. N would be −1, not +1. Furthermore, a triple bond is preferred as it satisfies octets.

choices.text: A triple bond between N and O; N has formal charge $0$ and O has formal charge $+1$, with the overall $+1$ charge on the ion
choices.explanation: NO⁺ has 10 valence electrons (5+6−1). A triple bond (6 e⁻) plus one lone pair on N (2 e⁻) and one lone pair on O (2 e⁻) = 10 e⁻. N: FC = 5−2−3 = 0; O: FC = 6−2−3 = +1. Total charge = +1. ✓

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following ionic compounds would be predicted to have the highest melting point based on lattice energy considerations?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$
choices.explanation: NaCl has 1+ and 1− charges and moderately sized ions; it has significant lattice energy (~787 kJ/mol) but not the highest among these options.

choices.text: $\text{CaCl}_2$
choices.explanation: CaCl₂ has a 2+ cation and two 1− anions; the higher charge of Ca²⁺ increases lattice energy over NaCl, but the smaller charges on each anion limit it compared to CaO.

choices.text: $\text{MgO}$
choices.explanation: MgO has Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ ions, both with charge 2. The high charges and small ionic radii of both ions produce very large lattice energy (~3795 kJ/mol) and the highest melting point (~2852°C) among these options.

choices.text: $\text{KBr}$
choices.explanation: KBr has 1+ and 1− charges with relatively large ionic radii; it has the lowest lattice energy among these options (~689 kJ/mol).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Acetylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$) has a linear structure with a carbon–carbon triple bond. Ethylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$) has a planar structure with a carbon–carbon double bond. Ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) is the saturated equivalent with a carbon–carbon single bond.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the hybridization of carbon in each molecule and explains the change in hybridization?

choices.text: Acetylene: $sp^3$; Ethylene: $sp^2$; Ethane: $sp$
choices.explanation: This is the reverse order. More pi bonds require fewer hybrid orbitals and lower hybridization; acetylene (triple bond) has $sp$ hybridized carbons, not $sp^3$.

choices.text: Acetylene: $sp$; Ethylene: $sp^2$; Ethane: $sp^3$
choices.explanation: As bond order decreases from triple to double to single, more sigma bonds form and hybridization increases. Acetylene C atoms form 2 sigma bonds ($sp$); ethylene C atoms form 3 sigma bonds ($sp^2$); ethane C atoms form 4 sigma bonds ($sp^3$).

choices.text: All three molecules have $sp^3$ hybridized carbons because carbon always forms 4 bonds
choices.explanation: Carbon forms 4 bonds total but the bonds differ: pi bonds use unhybridized p orbitals, not hybrid orbitals. Only single-bond carbons are $sp^3$.

choices.text: Acetylene: $sp^2$; Ethylene: $sp^3$; Ethane: $sp$
choices.explanation: Ethane, with four single bonds per carbon, is $sp^3$; acetylene, with a triple bond, is $sp$; ethylene is $sp^2$. This order is scrambled.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why the $\text{O–O}$ bond in hydrogen peroxide ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$, bond order = 1) is weaker than the $\text{O=O}$ bond in molecular oxygen ($\text{O}_2$, bond order = 2)?

choices.text: Single bonds always have lower bond energy than double bonds because they involve fewer shared electrons
choices.explanation: The O–O single bond (bond order 1, ~146 kJ/mol) is significantly weaker than the O=O double bond (bond order 2, ~498 kJ/mol) because fewer electrons are shared between the oxygen nuclei, resulting in less attraction holding the atoms together.

choices.text: The hydrogen atoms in $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ donate electrons to oxygen, weakening the O–O bond
choices.explanation: Hydrogen is less electronegative than oxygen; hydrogen does not donate electrons to oxygen. The weaker O–O bond in H₂O₂ is due to lower bond order, not hydrogen donation.

choices.text: The lone pairs on oxygen atoms in $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ repel each other, but this does not affect bond strength
choices.explanation: Lone pair–lone pair repulsion on adjacent oxygens in H₂O₂ does destabilize the O–O bond and is a contributing factor, but the primary reason for lower bond energy is the lower bond order (1 vs 2).

choices.text: Double bonds have more electrons, increasing nuclear repulsion and weakening the bond
choices.explanation: More shared electrons in a double bond increase the attractive force between nuclei, strengthening the bond, not weakening it.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student tests an unknown solid: it is malleable, has a melting point of 450°C, and conducts electricity in both solid and liquid states. Based on the table, which solid type does the unknown most resemble?

choices.text: Ionic solid ($\text{NaCl}$)
choices.explanation: Ionic solids are hard and brittle, do not conduct in the solid state, and typically have higher melting points. The malleability and solid-state conductivity rule out an ionic solid.

choices.text: Molecular solid ($\text{I}_2$)
choices.explanation: Molecular solids are soft and do not conduct electricity in any state; the unknown conducts in both solid and liquid states.

choices.text: Metallic solid ($\text{Cu}$)
choices.explanation: Metallic solids are malleable and conduct electricity in both solid and liquid states due to mobile delocalized electrons. The 450°C melting point is consistent with a lower-melting metal (e.g., tin melts at 232°C, lead at 328°C, zinc at 420°C).

choices.text: Covalent network solid ($\text{SiO}_2$)
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids are very hard (not malleable) and do not conduct electricity; the unknown's malleability and conductivity rule out a covalent network solid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
